revision.text:
{{Infobox Ägyptische Gottheit |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleres Reich]] |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Übersetzung nicht möglich]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[Griechisch-römische Zeit|Gr.-röm. Zeit]] }} '''Kenmet''' (auch '''Kenmut''') ist als [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] [[Schlangen]]gottheit des [[Himmelskugel|Himmels]] erstmals in den [[Sargtexte]]n des [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reiches]] in den [[Sargtexte]]n belegt. Zugleich tritt Kenmet im [[Nutbuch]] als gleichnamiger [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] in der Funktion des für die [[Nilschwemme]] verantwortlichen [[Sternbild]]es auf. == Hintergrund == === Darstellungen und Funktionen === [[Datei:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Darstellung der Isis im Neuen Reich]] Im Mittleren Reich ist Kenmet [[Ikonografie|ikonografisch]] sowohl als aufgerichtete Schlange dargestellt, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wird, wie auch als Schlange mit [[Falken]]- oder Menschenkopf. Gleichzeitig verkörpert Kenmet die Eigenschaften eines [[Schafe|Widders]] (''Ba''), des [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] und der [[Isis (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Isis]]. Während der [[Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|dritten Zwischenzeit]] verehrt Kenmet die Tätigkeiten des [[Amun-Re]] im Zusammenhang seiner Erscheinungsform als [[Re-Harachte]], [[Harachte]], [[Chepre|Chepri]], [[Atum]] und [[Harmachis]]. In der [[Griechisch-römische Zeit|griechisch-römischen Zeit]] wird Kenmets Funktion der Isis mit den Charaktereigenschaften der [[Sopdet]] als Trägerin vom [[Auge des Re]] gleichgesetzt. === Mythologische Aspekte === Im [[Neues Reich|Neuen Reich]] stellt Kenmet mit den zwei himmlischen Schildkröten das ''Leben des [[Horus]]'' dar. Kenmet ist die Gottheit, die am [[Achet#Datierungen|15. Achet IV]] auszieht, um ''die Zeit des [[Osiris]] zu bringen''. In der griechisch-römischen Zeit verkörperte Kenmet als Horus von [[Letopolis]] (''Hem'', nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) die Zeit der Aussaat, weshalb Kenmet im [[Opfer (Religion)|Opferkult]] auch als Empfänger genannt wurde. Ergänzend war Kenmet in dieser Epoche [[Chronokrat (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Chronokrat]] der ersten Nachtstunde und stand als ''Ausgerüsteter an der Spitze der göttlichen [[Stern]]e bei ihrem Umlauf''. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der ägyptischen Götter]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]] u.a.: ''[[Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen]]''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, S. 290. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Gottheit]]

Zugleich tritt Kenmet im [[Nutbuch]] als gleichnamiger [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] in der Funktion des für die [[Nilschwemme]] verantwortlichen [[Sternbild]]es auf. == Hintergrund == === Darstellungen und Funktionen === [[Datei:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Kenmet ist die Gottheit, die am [[Achet#Datierungen|15. Achet IV]] auszieht, um ''die Zeit des [[Osiris]] zu bringen''. In der griechisch-römischen Zeit verkörperte Kenmet als Horus von [[Letopolis]] (''Hem'', nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) die Zeit der Aussaat, weshalb Kenmet im [[Opfer (Religion)|Opferkult]] auch als Empfänger genannt wurde. Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Übersetzung nicht möglich]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[Griechisch-römische Zeit|Gr.-röm. Zeit]] }} '''Kenmet''' (auch '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') ist als [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] [[Schlangen]]gottheit des [[Himmelskugel|Himmels]] erstmals in den [[Sargtexte]]n des [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reiches]] sowie den zugehörigen [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en belegt. Zugleich tritt Kenmet im [[Nutbuch]] als gleichnamiger [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] in der Funktion des für die [[Nilschwemme]] verantwortlichen [[Sternbild]]es auf. Die in der [[Sekundärliteratur]] öfter hergestellte Verbindung der Namen ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (und ähnlich) mit der Gottheit [[Chnum]] ist nicht haltbar, da beispielsweise das auf [[Gemmen]] vorkommende sogenannte ''Chnouphiszeichen'' dem [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] zuzuordnen ist. == Hintergrund == === Darstellungen und Funktionen === [[Datei:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Darstellung der Isis im Neuen Reich]] Im Mittleren Reich ist Kenmet [[Ikonografie|ikonografisch]] sowohl als aufgerichtete Schlange dargestellt, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wird, wie auch als Schlange mit [[Falken]]- oder Menschenkopf. Gleichzeitig verkörpert Kenmet die Eigenschaften eines [[Schafe|Widders]] (''Ba''), des [[Re (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] und der [[Isis (Ägyptische Mythologie)|Isis]]. Während der [[Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|dritten Zwischenzeit]] verehrt Kenmet die Tätigkeiten des [[Amun-Re]] im Zusammenhang seiner Erscheinungsform als [[Re-Harachte]], [[Harachte]], [[Chepre|Chepri]], [[Atum]] und [[Harmachis]]. In der [[Griechisch-römische Zeit|griechisch-römischen Zeit]] wird Kenmets Funktion der Isis mit den Charaktereigenschaften der [[Sopdet]] als Trägerin vom [[Auge des Re]] gleichgesetzt. === Mythologische Aspekte === Im [[Neues Reich|Neuen Reich]] stellt Kenmet mit den zwei himmlischen Schildkröten das ''Leben des [[Horus]]'' dar. Kenmet ist die Gottheit, die am [[Achet#Datierungen|15. Achet IV]] auszieht, um ''die Zeit des [[Osiris]] zu bringen'' ([[Jahreszeit]] [[Oktober]]). In der griechisch-römischen Zeit verkörperte Kenmet als Horus von [[Letopolis]] (''Hem'', nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) die Zeit der Aussaat, weshalb Kenmet im [[Opfer (Religion)|Opferkult]] auch als Empfänger genannt wurde. Ergänzend war Kenmet in dieser Epoche [[Chronokrat]] der ersten Nachtstunde und stand als ''Ausgerüsteter an der Spitze der göttlichen [[Stern]]e bei ihrem Umlauf''. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der ägyptischen Götter]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]] u.a.: ''[[Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen]]''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, S. 290. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5 * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * [[Joachim Friedrich Quack]], Kim Ryholt: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Kopenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Gottheit]]

''[Übersetzung nicht möglich]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[Griechisch-römische Zeit|Gr.-röm. Zeit]] }} '''Kenmet''' (auch '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') ist als [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] [[Schlangen]]gottheit des [[Himmelskugel|Himmels]] erstmals in den [[Sargtexte]]n des [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reiches]] sowie den zugehörigen [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en belegt. Zugleich tritt Kenmet im [[Nutbuch]] als gleichnamiger [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] in der Funktion des für die [[Nilschwemme]] verantwortlichen [[Sternbild]]es auf. Die in der [[Sekundärliteratur]] öfter hergestellte Verbindung der Namen ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (und ähnlich) mit der Gottheit [[Chnum]] ist nicht haltbar, da beispielsweise das auf [[Gemme]]n vorkommende sogenannte ''Chnouphiszeichen'' dem [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] zuzuordnen ist.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. Ergänzend war Kenmet in dieser Epoche [[Chronokrat]] der ersten Nachtstunde und stand als ''Ausgerüsteter an der Spitze der göttlichen [[Stern]]e bei ihrem Umlauf''. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der ägyptischen Götter]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]] u.a.: ''[[Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen]]''. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Darstellung der Isis im Neuen Reich]] Im Mittleren Reich ist Kenmet [[Ikonografie|ikonografisch]] sowohl als aufgerichtete Schlange dargestellt, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wird, wie auch als Schlange mit [[Falken]]- oder Menschenkopf. Gleichzeitig verkörpert Kenmet die Eigenschaften eines [[Schafe|Widders]] (''Ba''), des [[Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] und der [[Isis]]. Während der [[Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|dritten Zwischenzeit]] verehrt Kenmet die Tätigkeiten des [[Amun-Re]] im Zusammenhang seiner Erscheinungsform als [[Re-Harachte]], [[Harachte]], [[Chepre|Chepri]], [[Atum]] und [[Harmachis]]. In der [[Griechisch-römische Zeit|griechisch-römischen Zeit]] wird Kenmets Funktion der Isis mit den Charaktereigenschaften der [[Sopdet]] als Trägerin vom [[Auge des Re]] gleichgesetzt. === Mythologische Aspekte === Im [[Neues Reich|Neuen Reich]] stellt Kenmet mit den zwei himmlischen Schildkröten das ''Leben des [[Horus]]'' dar. Kenmet ist die Gottheit, die am [[Achet#Datierungen|15. Achet IV]] auszieht, um ''die Zeit des [[Osiris]] zu bringen'' ([[Jahreszeit]] [[Oktober]]). In der griechisch-römischen Zeit verkörperte Kenmet als Horus von [[Letopolis]] (''Hem'', nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) die Zeit der Aussaat, weshalb Kenmet im [[Opfer (Religion)|Opferkult]] auch als Empfänger genannt wurde. Ergänzend war Kenmet in dieser Epoche [[Chronokrat]] der ersten Nachtstunde und stand als ''Ausgerüsteter an der Spitze der göttlichen [[Stern]]e bei ihrem Umlauf''. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der ägyptischen Götter]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]] u. a.: ''[[Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen]]''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, S. 290. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * [[Joachim Friedrich Quack]], Kim Ryholt: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Kopenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Gottheit]] [[Kategorie:Schlangengottheit]]

{{Infobox Ägyptische Gottheit |TITEL = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleres Reich]] |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Übersetzung nicht möglich]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|Gr.-röm. Zeit]] }} '''Kenmet''' (auch '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') ist als [[Altes Ägypten|altägyptische]] [[Schlangen]]gottheit des [[Himmelskugel|Himmels]] erstmals in den [[Sargtexte]]n des [[Mittleres Reich (Ägypten)|Mittleren Reiches]] sowie den zugehörigen [[Diagonalsternuhr]]en belegt. Zugleich tritt Kenmet im [[Nutbuch]] als gleichnamiger [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] in der Funktion des für die [[Nilschwemme]] verantwortlichen [[Sternbild]]es auf. Die in der [[Sekundärliteratur]] öfter hergestellte Verbindung der Namen ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (und ähnlich) mit der Gottheit [[Chnum]] ist nicht haltbar, da beispielsweise das auf [[Gemme]]n vorkommende sogenannte ''Chnouphiszeichen'' dem [[Kenmet (Dekan)|Dekan Kenmet]] zuzuordnen ist.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. S. 59. == Hintergrund == === Darstellungen und Funktionen === [[Datei:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Darstellung der Isis im Neuen Reich]] Im Mittleren Reich ist Kenmet [[Ikonografie|ikonografisch]] sowohl als aufgerichtete Schlange dargestellt, die von drei kleineren Schlangen gekreuzt wird, wie auch als Schlange mit [[Falken]]- oder Menschenkopf. Gleichzeitig verkörpert Kenmet die Eigenschaften eines [[Schafe|Widders]] (''Ba''), des [[Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|Re]] und der [[Isis]]. Während der [[Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|dritten Zwischenzeit]] verehrt Kenmet die Tätigkeiten des [[Amun-Re]] im Zusammenhang seiner Erscheinungsform als [[Re-Harachte]], [[Harachte]], [[Chepre|Chepri]], [[Atum]] und [[Harmachis]]. In der [[Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|griechisch-römischen Zeit]] wird Kenmets Funktion der Isis mit den Charaktereigenschaften der [[Sopdet]] als Trägerin vom [[Auge des Re]] gleichgesetzt. === Mythologische Aspekte === Im [[Neues Reich|Neuen Reich]] stellt Kenmet mit den zwei himmlischen Schildkröten das ''Leben des [[Horus]]'' dar. Kenmet ist die Gottheit, die am [[Achet#Datierungen|15. Achet IV]] auszieht, um ''die Zeit des [[Osiris]] zu bringen'' ([[Jahreszeit]] [[Oktober]]). In der griechisch-römischen Zeit verkörperte Kenmet als Horus von [[Letopolis]] (''Hem'', nordwestlich von [[Memphis (Ägypten)|Memphis]]) die Zeit der Aussaat, weshalb Kenmet im [[Opfer (Religion)|Opferkult]] auch als Empfänger genannt wurde. Ergänzend war Kenmet in dieser Epoche [[Chronokrat]] der ersten Nachtstunde und stand als ''Ausgerüsteter an der Spitze der göttlichen [[Stern]]e bei ihrem Umlauf''. == Siehe auch == * [[Liste der ägyptischen Götter]] == Literatur == * [[Christian Leitz]] u. a.: ''[[Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen]]''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, S. 290. * [[Alexandra von Lieven]]: ''Grundriss des Laufes der Sterne – Das sogenannte Nutbuch''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies (u. a.), Kopenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna – Eine Fallstudie zur religiösen Astronomie in Ägypten am Beispiel der kosmologischen Decken- und Architravinschriften im Tempel von Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * [[Joachim Friedrich Quack]], Kim Ryholt: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Kopenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == Einzelnachweise == [[Kategorie:Ägyptische Gottheit]] [[Kategorie:Schlangengottheit]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Egyptian Deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Nile Flood|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Egyptian Painter around 1360 BC 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Re-Harachte|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Harachte|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[category:completion tier 1]][[ja:ケンメト]][[de:Kenmet]]

{{Infobox Egyptian Deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10

Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq]][[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]][[de:Kenmet]]

Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq]][[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]][[de:Kenmet]]

{{Infobox Egyptian Deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Nile Flood|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq]][[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]][[de:Kenmet]]

{{Infobox Egyptian Deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10

Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq]][[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]][[de:Kenmet]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]][[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]][[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]][[Category:qq]]

revision.text:
{{Infobox Egyptian Deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Nile Flood|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:pages which might possibly be incomplete but are most likely complete]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]]

''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代|12=draft}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト|12=draft}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球|12=draft}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の|12=draft}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計|12=draft}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書|12=draft}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫|12=draft}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献|12=draft}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム|12=draft}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石|12=draft}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊|12=draft}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ|12=draft}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期|12=draft}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ|12=draft}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト|12=draft}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト|12=draft}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目|12=draft}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|12=draft}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|12=draft}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:Qq]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:I think I cleared all the incomplete or blank pages earlier]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]]

Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft}}). Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]]

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Infobox Egyptian Deity" to "Infobox deity"
revision.text:
{{Infobox deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|12=draft}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代|12=draft}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト|12=draft}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球|12=draft}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の|12=draft}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計|12=draft}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書|12=draft}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫|12=draft}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献|12=draft}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム|12=draft}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石|12=draft}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊|12=draft}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ|12=draft}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期|12=draft}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ|12=draft}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト|12=draft}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト|12=draft}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目|12=draft}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|12=draft}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|12=draft}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]]

Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:New]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]]

Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]] [[sn: failed]] [[Category:Ja import failed]]

Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ja import failed]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Infobox deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|12=draft}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代|12=draft}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト|12=draft}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球|12=draft}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の|12=draft}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計|12=draft}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書|12=draft}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫|12=draft}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献|12=draft}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム|12=draft}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石|12=draft}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊|12=draft}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ|12=draft}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期|12=draft}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ|12=draft}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト|12=draft}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト|12=draft}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目|12=draft}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|12=draft}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|12=draft}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ja import failed]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]][[Category:qq]] }} [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]] [[sn: failed]]

''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代|12=draft}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト|12=draft}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球|12=draft}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の|12=draft}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計|12=draft}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書|12=draft}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫|12=draft}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献|12=draft}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム|12=draft}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石|12=draft}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊|12=draft}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ|12=draft}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期|12=draft}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ|12=draft}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト|12=draft}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト|12=draft}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目|12=draft}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|12=draft}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|12=draft}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊|12=draft|12=draft}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ|12=draft|12=draft}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス|12=draft|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ|12=draft|12=draft}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト|12=draft|12=draft}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目|12=draft|12=draft}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] [[Category:Ja import failed]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]] [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn: failed]]

{{Infobox deity |TITLE = Kenmet |NAME = V31:N35-X1 G14-N14 |NAME-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|12=draft|12=draft}} |NAME2 = V31:N35-T34-G17-N14-X1:H8-C10
Kenmet
''Knmt''
''[Translation not possible]'' |NAME2-ERWEITERT = {{ill|Egypt in Greco-Roman Period|de|Gr.-röm. Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代|12=draft|12=draft}} }} '''Kenmet''' (also '''Kenmut, Chnoubis, Chnoumis, Chnouphis, Knuphis''') is attested as an {{ill|Ancient Egypt|de|Altes Ägypten|ja|古代エジプト|12=draft|12=draft}}ian {{ill|Serpent Deity|de|Schlangengottheit|ja|蛇神|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Celestial Sphere|de|Himmelskugel|ja|天球|12=draft|12=draft}}, first in the {{ill|Coffin Texts|de|Sargtexte|ja|棺文|12=draft|12=draft}} of the {{ill|Middle Kingdom (Egypt)|de|Mittleres Reich|ja|中王国|lt=of the Middle Kingdom|lt_de=Mittleren Reiches|lt_ja=中王国の|12=draft|12=draft}} as well as in the associated {{ill|Diagonal Star Clock|de|Diagonalsternuhr|ja|対角星時計|12=draft|12=draft}}. At the same time, Kenmet appears in the {{ill|Book of Nut|de|Nutbuch|ja|ヌトの書|12=draft|12=draft}} as the eponymous {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft|12=draft}} in the role of the constellation responsible for the {{ill|Flooding of the Nile|de|Nilschwemme|ja|ナイルの氾濫|12=draft|12=draft}}. The association frequently made in the {{ill|Secondary Literature|de|Sekundärliteratur|ja|二次文献|12=draft|12=draft}} between the names ''Chnoubis, Chnoumis'' (and similar) and the deity {{ill|Chnum|de|Chnum|ja|クム|12=draft|12=draft}} is untenable, since, for example, the so‐called ''Chnouphis sign'' found on {{ill|Gemmae|de|Gemme|ja|宝石|12=draft|12=draft}} can be attributed to {{ill|Decan Kenmet|de|Kenmet (Dekan)|ja|ケンメト（デカン）|12=draft|12=draft}}.Alexandra von Lieven: ''Der Himmel über Esna''. p. 59. == Background == === Representations and Functions === [[File:Ägyptischer Maler um 1360 v. Chr. 001.jpg|thumb|Depiction of Isis in the New Kingdom]] In the Middle Kingdom, Kenmet is iconographically represented both as an erect snake, crossed by three smaller snakes, and as a snake with a falcon or human head. At the same time, Kenmet embodies the attributes of a {{ill|Ram|de|Widders|ja|牡羊|12=draft|12=draft}} (''Ba''), of {{ill|Re (Egyptian mythology)|de|Re (ägyptische Mythologie)|ja|レ|12=draft|12=draft}}, and of {{ill|Isis|de|Isis|ja|イシス|12=draft|12=draft}}. During the {{ill|Third Intermediate Period (Egypt)|de|Dritte Zwischenzeit (Ägypten)|ja|第三中間期|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kenmet venerates the activities of {{ill|Amun-Re|de|Amun-Re|ja|アメン・レ|12=draft|12=draft}} in connection with his manifestation as {{ill|Ra-Horakhty|de|Re-Harachte|ja|レ＝ハラハテ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Horakhty|de|Harachte|ja|ハラハテ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Chepri|de|Chepre|ja|チェプレ|lt=Chepri|lt_de=Chepri|lt_ja=チェプレ|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Atum|de|Atum|ja|アトゥム|12=draft|12=draft}}, and {{ill|Harmachis|de|Harmachis|ja|ハルマキス|12=draft|12=draft}}. In the {{ill|Egypt in the Greco-Roman Period|de|Ägypten in griechisch-römischer Zeit|ja|ギリシャ・ローマ時代のエジプト|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kenmet's function as Isis is equated with the character traits of {{ill|Sopdet|de|Sopdet|ja|ソプデト|12=draft|12=draft}} as the bearer of the {{ill|Eye of Re|de|Auge des Re|ja|レの目|12=draft|12=draft}}. === Mythological Aspects === In the {{ill|New Kingdom|de|Neues Reich|ja|新王国|12=draft|12=draft}}, Kenmet, together with the two celestial turtles, represents the ''Life of {{ill|Horus|de|Horus|ja|ホルス|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Kenmet is the deity who departs on {{ill|Achet|de|Achet|ja|アヘト|lt=15. Achet IV|lt_de=15. Achet IV|lt_ja=15.アヘトIV|12=draft|12=draft}} to bring "the time of {{ill|Osiris|de|Osiris|ja|オシリス|12=draft|12=draft}}" ({{ill|Season|de|Jahreszeit|ja|季節|12=draft|12=draft}} {{ill|October|de|Oktober|ja|10月|12=draft|12=draft}}). In the Greco-Roman period, Kenmet, as the Horus of {{ill|Letopolis|de|Letopolis|ja|レトポリス|12=draft|12=draft}} (''Hem'', northwest of {{ill|Memphis (Egypt)|de|Memphis (Ägypten)|ja|メンフィス|12=draft|12=draft}}), embodied the season of sowing, which is why Kenmet was also designated as a recipient in the {{ill|Sacrificial Cult|de|Opfer (Religion)|ja|犠牲祭|12=draft|12=draft}}. Additionally, in this epoch, Kenmet was the {{ill|Chronocrat|de|Chronokrat|ja|クロノクラート|12=draft|12=draft}} of the first hour of the night and stood as the "armored one at the head of the divine {{ill|Star|de|Stern|ja|星|12=draft|12=draft}}s during their rotation". == See also == * {{ill|List of Egyptian Deities|de|Liste der ägyptischen Götter|ja|エジプトの神々の一覧|12=draft|12=draft}} == Literature == * {{ill|Christian Leitz|de|Christian Leitz|ja|クリスチャン・ライツ|12=draft|12=draft}} et al.: ''{{ill|Lexicon of Egyptian Deities and Deity Names|de|Lexikon der ägyptischen Götter und Götterbezeichnungen|ja|エジプト神と神名の辞典|12=draft|12=draft}}''. Peeters, Leuven 2002, ISBN 90-429-1146-8, p. 290. * {{ill|Alexandra von Lieven|de|Alexandra von Lieven|ja|アレクサンドラ・フォン・リーベン|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Outline of the Course of the Stars – The so‑called Book of Nut''. The Carsten Niebuhr Institute of Ancient Eastern Studies et al., Copenhagen 2007, ISBN 978-87-635-0406-5. * Alexandra von Lieven: ''The Sky over Esna – A Case Study on Religious Astronomy in Egypt as Exemplified by the Cosmological Ceiling and Architrave Inscriptions in the Temple of Esna''. Harrassowitz, Wiesbaden 2000, ISBN 3-447-04324-5. * {{ill|Joachim Friedrich Quack|de|Joachim Friedrich Quack|ja|ヨアヒム・フリードリヒ・クアック|12=draft|12=draft}}, {{ill|Kim Ryholt|de|Kim Ryholt|ja|キム・リヨルト|12=draft|12=draft}}: ''Hieratic texts from the collection - The Carlsberg papyri 7 -''. Museum Tusculanum Press, Copenhagen 2006, ISBN 87-635-0405-7. == References == {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| }} {{draft categories| [[Category:Egyptian Deity]] [[Category:Serpent Deity]] [[Category:Completion tier 1]] }} [[sn:Kenmet (Egyptian mythology)]] [[de:Kenmet (ägyptische Mythologie)]] [[ja:ケンメト]] [[de:Kenmet]] [[sn: failed]]
